subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: Time can be measured.

subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: Personal history can be shared through stories and pictures.

subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: Heritage is reflected through diverse cultures and is shown through the arts, customs, traditions, family celebrations and language.

subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: Symbols and practices of the United States include the flag, Pledge of Allegiance and the National Anthem. Other nations are represented by symbols and practices too.

subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: Terms related to direction and distance, as well as symbols and landmarks, can be used to talk about the relative location of familiar places.

subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: Models and maps represent real places.

subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: Humans depend on and impact the physical environment in order to supply food, clothing and shelter.

subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: Individuals are unique but share common characteristics of multiple groups.

subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: Individuals share responsibilities and take action toward the achievement of common goals in homes, schools and communities.

subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: The purpose of rules and authority figures is to provide order, security and safety in the home, school and community.

subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: Individuals have many wants and make decisions to satisfy those wants. These decisions impact others.

subject: social_studies
theme: A Child’s Place in Time and Space
description: Goods are objects that can satisfy an individual’s wants. Services are actions that can satisfy individual’s wants.

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: Time can be divided into categories (e.g., months of the year, past, present and future).

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: Photographs, letters, artifacts and books can be used to learn about the past.

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: The ways basic human needs are met have changed over time.

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: Maps can be used to locate and identify places.

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: Places are distinctive because of their physical characteristics (land forms and bodies of water) and human characteristics (structures built by people).

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: Families interact with the physical environment differently in different times and places.

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: Diverse cultural practices address basic human needs in various ways and may change over time

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: Individuals have responsibility to take action toward the achievement of common goals in homes, schools and communities and are accountable for those actions.

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: Collaboration requires group members to respect the rights and opinions of others.

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: Rules exist in different settings. The principles of fairness should guide rules and the consequences for breaking rules.

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: Wants are unlimited and resources are limited. Individuals make choices because they cannot have everything they want.

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: People produce and consume goods and services in the community.

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: People trade to obtain goods and services they want.

subject: social_studies
theme: Families Now and Long Ago, Near and Far
description: Currency is used as a means of economic exchange.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Time can be shown graphically on calendars and timelines.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Change over time can be shown with artifacts, maps, and photographs.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Science and technology have changed daily life.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Biographies can show how peoples’ actions have shaped the world in which we live.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Maps and their symbols, including cardinal directions, can be interpreted to answer questions about location of places.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: The work that people do is impacted by the distinctive human and physical characteristics in the place where they live.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Human activities alter the physical environment, both positively and negatively.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Cultures develop in unique ways, in part through the influence of the physical environment.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Interactions among cultures lead to sharing ways of life.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Respect for the rights of self and others includes making responsible choices and being accountable for personal actions.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Groups are accountable for choices they make and actions they take.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: There are different rules and laws that govern behavior in different settings.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Information displayed on bar graphs can be used to compare quantities.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Resources can be used in various ways.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: Most people around the world work in jobs in which they produce specific goods and services.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: People use money to buy and sell goods and services.

subject: social_studies
theme: People Working Together
description: People earn income by working.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Events in local history can be shown on timelines organized by years, decades and centuries.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Primary and secondary sources can be used to show change over time.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Local communities change over time.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Physical and political maps have distinctive characteristics and purposes. Places can be located on a map by using the title, key, alphanumeric grid and cardinal directions.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Daily life is influenced by the agriculture, industry and natural resources in different communities.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Evidence of positive and negative human modification of the environment can be observed in the local community.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Systems of transportation and communication move people, products and ideas from place to place.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Communities may include diverse cultural groups.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Members of local communities have rights and responsibilities.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Individuals make the community a better place by taking action to solve problems in a way that promotes the common good.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Laws are rules which apply to all people in a community and describe ways people are expected to behave. Laws promote order and security, provide public services and protect the rights of individuals in the local community.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Governments have authority to make and enforce laws.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: The structure of local governments may differ from one community to another.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Line graphs are used to show changes in data over time.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Both positive and negative incentives affect individuals’ choices and behaviors.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Individuals must make decisions because of the scarcity of resources. Making a decision involves a trade-off.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: A consumer is a person whose wants are satisfied by using goods and services. A producer makes goods and/or provides services.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: A market is where buyers and sellers exchange goods and services.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: Making decisions involves weighing costs and benefits.

subject: social_studies
theme: Communities: Past and Present, Near and Far
description: A budget is a plan to help people make personal economic decisions for the present and future and to become more financially responsible.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: The order of significant events in Ohio and the United States can be shown on a timeline.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Primary and secondary sources can be used to create historical narratives.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Various groups of people have lived in Ohio over time including American Indians, migrating settlers and immigrants. Interactions among these groups have resulted in cooperation, conflict and compromise.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: The 13 colonies came together around a common cause of liberty and justice, uniting to fight for independence during the American Revolution and to form a new nation.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: The Northwest Ordinance incorporated democratic ideals into the territories. It provided a process for territories to become states and recognized them as equal to the other existing states.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Ongoing conflicts on the Ohio frontier with American Indians and Great Britain contributed to the United States’ involvement in the War of 1812.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Following the War of 1812, Ohio continued to play a key role in national conflicts including the anti-slavery movement and the Underground Railroad.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Many technological innovations that originated in Ohio benefitted the United States.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: A map scale and cardinal and intermediate directions can be used to describe the relative location of physical and human characteristics of Ohio and the United States.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: The economic development of the United States continues to influence and be influenced by agriculture, industry and natural resources in Ohio.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: The regions of the United States known as the North, South and West developed in the early 1800s largely based on their physical environments and economies.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: People have modified the environment throughout history resulting in both positive and negative consequences in Ohio and the United States.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: The population of the United States has changed over time, becoming more diverse (e.g., racial, ethnic, linguistic, religious). Ohio’s population has become increasingly reflective of the multicultural diversity of the United States.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Ohio’s location and its transportation systems continue to influence the movement of people, products and ideas in the United States.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Individuals have a variety of opportunities to act in and influence their state and national government. Citizens have both rights and responsibilities in Ohio and the United States.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Civic participation in a democratic society requires individuals to make informed and reasoned decisions by accessing, evaluating and using information effectively to engage in compromise.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Laws can protect rights, provide benefits and assign responsibilities.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: The U.S. Constitution establishes a system of limited government and protects citizens’ rights; five of these rights are addressed in the First Amendment.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: A constitution is a written plan for government. The Ohio Constitution and the U.S. Constitution separate the major responsibilities of government among three branches.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Tables and charts organize data in a variety of formats to help individuals understand information and issues.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Entrepreneurs in Ohio and the United States organize productive resources and take risks to make a profit and compete with other producers.

subject: social_studies
theme: Ohio in the United States
description: Saving a portion of income contributes to an individuals’ financial well-being. Individuals can reduce spending to save more of their income.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: Events can be arranged in order of occurrence using the conventions of B.C. and A.D. or B.C.E. and C.E.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: Early Indian civilizations (Maya, Inca, Aztec, Mississippian) existed in the Western Hemisphere prior to the arrival of Europeans. These civilizations had developed unique governments, social structures, religions, technologies, and agricultural practices.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: European exploration and colonization during the 1400s-1600s had lasting effects which can be used to understand the Western Hemisphere today.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: Geographic tools can be used to gather, process and report information about people, places and environments. Cartographers decide which information to include in maps.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: Latitude and longitude can be used to make observations about location and generalizations about climate

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: Regions can be determined using data related to various criteria including landform, climate, population, and cultural and economic characteristics.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: The variety of physical environments within the Western Hemisphere influences human activities. Likewise, human activities modify the physical environments.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: American Indians developed unique cultures with many different ways of life. American Indian tribes and nations can be classified into cultural groups based on geographic and cultural similarities.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: Political, environmental, social and economic factors cause people, products and ideas to move from place to place in the Western Hemisphere and results in diversity.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: The Western Hemisphere is culturally diverse (eg., language, food, religion, art, music) due to the influences and interactions of a variety of world cultures.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: Individuals can better understand public issues by gathering, interpreting and checking information for accuracy from multiple sources. Data can be displayed graphically to effectively and efficiently communicate information.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: Democracies, dictatorships and monarchies are categories for understanding the relationship between those in power or authority and citizens.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: Information displayed in circle graphs can be used to show relative proportions of segments of data to an entire body of data.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: The choices made by individuals and governments have both present and future consequences.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: The availability of productive resources (i.e., entrepreneurship, human resources, capital goods and natural resources) promotes specialization that could lead to trade.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: The availability of productive resources and the division of labor can have a positive or negative impact on productive capacity.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: Regions and countries become interdependent when they specialize in what they produce best and then trade with other regions to increase the amount and variety of goods and services available.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Western Hemisphere
description: Workers can improve their ability to earn income by gaining new knowledge, skills and experiences.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: Multiple tier timelines can be used to show relationships among events and places.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: Early civilizations (India, Egypt, China and Mesopotamia) had unique governments, economic systems, social structures, religions, technologies and agricultural practices and products. The cultural practices and products of these early civilizations can be used to help understand the Eastern Hemisphere today.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: Geographic tools can be used to gather, process and report information about people, places and environments. Cartographers decide which information to include and how it is displayed.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: Latitude and longitude can be used to identify absolute location.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: Regions can be determined, classified and compared using data related to various criteria including landform, climate, population, and cultural and economic characteristics

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: The variety of physical environments within the Eastern Hemisphere influences human activities. Likewise, human activities modify the physical environment.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: Political, environmental, social and economic factors cause people, products and ideas to move from place to place in the Eastern Hemisphere in the past and today.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: Diffusion of agricultural practices and products, technology, cultural practices and major world religions (Buddhism, Christianity, Hinduism, Islam and Judaism) impacted the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: Different perspectives on a topic can be obtained from a variety of historic and contemporary sources and used to effectively communicate and defend a claim based on evidence. Sources should be examined for accuracy and credibility.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: Governments can be categorized as monarchies, theocracies, dictatorships or democracies, but categories may overlap and labels may not accurately represent how governments function. The extent of citizens’ liberties and responsibilities varies according to limits on governmental authority.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: Economists compare data sets to draw conclusions about relationships among them.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: The choices made by individuals and governments have both present and future consequences. The evaluation of choices is relative and may differ across individuals and societies.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: The fundamental questions of economics include what to produce, how to produce and for whom to produce.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: When regions and/or countries specialize, global trade occurs.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: The interaction of supply and demand, influenced by competition, helps to determine price in a market. This interaction also determines the quantities of outputs produced and the quantities of productive resources (entrepreneurship, human resources, natural resources and capital) used.

subject: social_studies
theme: Regions and People of the Eastern Hemisphere
description: When selecting items to buy, individuals can weigh costs and benefits and compare the price and quality of available goods and services.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: Historians and archaeologists describe historical events and issues from the perspectives of people living at the time to avoid evaluating the past in terms of today’s norms and values.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: The civilizations that developed in Greece and Rome had an enduring impact on later civilizations. This legacy includes governance and law, engineering and technology, art and architecture, as well as literature and history. The Roman Empire also played an instrumental role in the spread of Christianity.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: The Roman Empire collapsed due to various internal and external factors (political, social and economic) which led to the development of feudalism and the manorial system in the region. The fall of Rome and later invasions also allowed for the creation of new empires in the region.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: The Mongols conquered much of Asia which led to unified states in China and Korea. Mongol failure to conquer Japan allowed a feudal system to persist.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: Achievements in medicine, science, mathematics and geography by the Islamic civilization dominated most of the Mediterranean after the decline of the Roman Empire. These achievements were introduced into Western Europe as a result of the Muslim conquests, Crusades and trade, influencing the European Renaissance.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: The decline of feudalism, the rise of nation-states and the Renaissance in Europe introduced revolutionary ideas, leading to cultural, scientific and social changes.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: The Reformation introduced changes in religion including the emergence of Protestant faiths and a decline in the political power and social influence of the Roman Catholic Church.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: Empires in Africa and Asia grew as commercial and cultural centers along trade routes.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: The advent of the trans-Saharan slave trade had profound effects on both West and Central Africa and the receiving societies.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: European economic and cultural influence dramatically increased through explorations, conquests and colonization.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: The Columbian exchange (i.e., the exchange of fauna, flora and pathogens) among previously unconnected parts of the world reshaped societies in ways still evident today.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: Maps and other geographic representations can be used to trace the development of human settlement over time.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: Geographic factors promote or impede the movement of people, products and ideas.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: Trade routes connecting Africa, Europe and Asia helped foster the spread of ideas, technology, goods and major world religions (Buddhism, Christianity, Hinduism, Islam and Judaism) that impacted the Eastern Hemisphere.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: Improvements in transportation, communication and technology have facilitated cultural diffusion among peoples around the world.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: Analyzing individual and group perspectives is essential to understanding historic and contemporary issues. Opportunities for civic engagement exist for students to connect real-world issues and events to classroom learning.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: Greek democracy and the Roman Republic were a radical departure from monarchy and theocracy, influencing the structure and function of modern democratic governments.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: With the decline of feudalism, consolidation of power resulted in the emergence of nation-states.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: Individuals, governments and businesses must analyze costs and benefits when making economic decisions. A cost-benefit analysis consists of determining the potential costs and benefits of an action and then balancing the costs against the benefits.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: The variability in the distribution of productive resources in the various regions of the world contributed to specialization, trade and interdependence.

subject: social_studies
theme: World Studies from 750 B.C. to 1600 A.D.: Ancient Greece to the First Global Age
description: The growth of cities and empires fostered the growth of markets. Market exchanges encouraged specialization and the transition from barter to monetary economies.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Primary and secondary sources are used to examine events from multiple perspectives and to present and defend a position.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: North America, originally inhabited by American Indians, was explored and colonized by Europeans for economic and religious reasons.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Competition for control of territory and resources in North America led to conflicts among colonizing powers.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: The practice of race-based slavery led to the forced migration of Africans to the American colonies and contributed to colonial economic development. Their knowledge, skills and traditions were essential to the development of the colonies.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: The ideas of the Enlightenment and dissatisfaction with colonial rule led English colonists to write the Declaration of Independence and launch the American Revolution.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Key events and significant figures in American history influenced the course and outcome of the American Revolution.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: The outcome of the American Revolution was national independence and new political, social and economic relationships for the American people.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Problems arising under the Articles of Confederation led to debate over the adoption of the U.S. Constitution.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Actions of early presidential administrations established a strong federal government, provided peaceful transitions of power and repelled a foreign invasion.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: The United States added to its territory through treaties and purchases.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Westward expansion contributed to economic and industrial development, debates over sectional issues, war with Mexico and the displacement of American Indians.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Disputes over the nature of federalism, complicated by economic developments in the United States, resulted in sectional issues, including slavery, which led to the American Civil War.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Key events and significant figures in American history influenced the course and outcome of the Civil War.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: The Reconstruction period resulted in changes to the U.S. Constitution, an affirmation of federal authority and lingering social and political differences.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Modern and historical maps and other geographic tools are used to analyze how historic events are shaped by geography.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: The availability of natural resources contributed to the geographic and economic expansion of the United States, sometimes resulting in unintended environmental consequences.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: The movement of people, products and ideas resulted in new patterns of settlement and land use that influenced the political and economic development of the United States.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Cultural biases, stereotypes and prejudices had social, political and economic consequences for minority groups and the population as a whole.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Americans began to develop a unique national identity among diverse regional and cultural populations based on democratic ideals.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Active participation in social and civic groups can lead to the attainment of individual and public goals.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Informed citizens understand how media and communication technology influence public opinion.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: The U.S. Constitution established a federal republic, providing a framework for a national government with elected representatives, separation of powers, and checks and balances.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: The U.S. Constitution protects citizens’ rights by limiting the powers of government.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Choices made by individuals, businesses and governments have both present and future consequences.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: The Industrial Revolution fundamentally changed the means of production as a result of improvements in technology, use of new power resources, the advent of interchangeable parts and the shift from craftwork to factory work.

subject: social_studies
theme: U.S. Studies from 1492 to 1877: Exploration Through Reconstruction
description: Governments can impact markets by means of spending, regulations, taxes and trade barriers.
